---
title: 'Myside bias undermines online content moderation'
subtitle: '<small>Content Moderation, Free Speech & Democracy | TUM Content Moderation Lab</small>'
author: 'Simon Munzert <span style="color:#ff0000;">&middot;</span> Hertie School, Berlin  <span style="color:#ff0000;">&middot;</span> Together with Julia Ellingwood, Lisa Oswald, Richard Traunmüller'
date: "<br>Apr 23, 2026<br><br><br>
<br>
<img src='hertieschool-neg.svg' style='height:25px; margin-right: 10px'>
<img src='kcl-logo.png' style='height:25px; margin-right: 10px'>
<img src='mpi-logo.png' style='height:25px; margin-right: 10px'>
<img src='mannheim-logo.png' style='height:25px; margin-right: 285px'>
Co-funded by: 
<img src='facebook-research-logo.png' style='height:25px'>
"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    includes:
      after_body: "add_hertie_logo.html"
      in_header: "my_header.html"
    css: [default, metropolis, metropolis-fonts, 'simons-touch.css'] 
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
      hash: true
---
class: inverse, center, middle


```{css, echo=FALSE} 
@media print { # print out incremental slides; see https://stackoverflow.com/questions/56373198/get-xaringan-incremental-animations-to-print-to-pdf/56374619#56374619
  .has-continuation {
    display: block !important;
  }
}
```

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# figures formatting setup
options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE)
library(knitr)
opts_chunk$set(
  prompt = T,
  fig.align="center", #fig.width=6, fig.height=4.5, 
  # out.width="748px", #out.length="520.75px",
  dpi=300, #fig.path='Figs/',
  cache=T, #echo=F, warning=F, message=F
  engine.opts = list(bash = "-l")
  )

## Next hook based on this SO answer: https://stackoverflow.com/a/39025054
knit_hooks$set(
  prompt = function(before, options, envir) {
    options(
      prompt = if (options$engine %in% c('sh','bash')) '$ ' else 'R> ',
      continue = if (options$engine %in% c('sh','bash')) '$ ' else '+ '
      )
})

library(tidyverse)
library(hrbrthemes)
library(fontawesome)
```

<style>

li {
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

ul {
  text-indent: -1.5em;
  list-style: none;
}

ul li::before {
  content: "";
  background: url('megaphone.png') no-repeat center;
  background-size: contain;
  width: 20px;
  height: 20px;
  flex-shrink: 0; /* Prevents image from resizing */
  display: inline-block;
  margin-right: 10px;
  position: relative;
  top: 0px; /* Moves image slightly down */
}
</style>


# Motivation
<html><div style='float:left'></div><hr color='#EB811B' size=1px style="width:1000px; margin:auto;"/></html>



---
# Content moderation is big and... bad?

.pull-left[

- Platforms moderate content opaquely, often without oversight ([Gillespie 2018](https://yalebooks.yale.edu/book/9780300261431/custodians-of-the-internet/))
- Content moderation with commercial services is **massive** (e.g., 500m requests per day to JigSaw's Perspective API in 2021; 200m weekly users of OpenAI's Moderation API)
- **Content Moderation APIs** by Google, Microsoft, Amazon, OpenAI, and others are supposedly trained on human labeled data but produce lots of questionable decisions ([Hartmann et al. 2025](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01623); FPR/FNR up to 75%, ACC as low as 60% in balanced toxic/non-toxic samples) 
]

.pull-right[
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/chi-lost-in-moderation-1.png" style="width:90%">
</div>
]

---
# Why hate speech moderation at scale is so challenging

.pull-left-small2[
## It's a hard problem 

- **Volume and velocity of content**  
  - Billions of posts per day  
  - Error amplification at scale
- **Adversarial users**  
  - Coded language, meme shifts  
  - Evasion of detection systems
- **Context is everything**  
  - Meaning depends on intent  
  - Hard for AI to judge nuance
- **Legal and reputational constraints**  
  - Balance free speech vs harm  
  - Laws differ across countries  
]

--

.pull-right-wide2[
## And we might not even agree on what the problem is...

- **The fundamental subjectivity of hate speech**  
  - Likely varies by culture, context of speech, and individual prefs
  - [Why it's impossible to agree on what's allowed](https://danluu.com/impossible-agree/) (Dan Luu) and the ["No vehicles in the park" rule](https://novehiclesinthepark.com/)
  
]


---
# Why hate speech moderation at scale is so challenging

.pull-left-small2[
## It's a hard problem 

- **Volume and velocity of content**  
  - Billions of posts per day  
  - Error amplification at scale
- **Adversarial users**  
  - Coded language, meme shifts  
  - Evasion of detection systems
- **Context is everything**  
  - Meaning depends on intent  
  - Hard for AI to judge nuance
- **Legal and reputational constraints**  
  - Balance free speech vs harm  
  - Laws differ across countries  
]

.pull-right-wide2[
## And we might not even agree on what the problem is...

- **The fundamental subjectivity of hate speech**  
  - Likely varies by culture, context of speech, and individual prefs
  - [Why it's impossible to agree on what's allowed](https://danluu.com/impossible-agree/) (Dan Luu) and the ["No vehicles in the park" rule](https://novehiclesinthepark.com/)
  
## ... and how it should be tackled

<div align="center">
<br>
<img src="figures/barplot-contentregulation.png" style="width:90%">
</div>
]


---
# What drives disagreement in moderation judgments?

.pull-left[

## Judgments demonstrably structured by:

- **Cultural norms** (Jiang et al. 2021)
- **Moral values** (Davani et al. 2024)
- **Racial attitudes** (Sap et al. 2022)
- **Group identity** (Das et al. 2024, Giorgi et al. 2025)

## Two known sources of bias

**Identity bias:** judgments shaped by demographic overlap (gender, race, religion) between annotator and speaker/target (Das et al. 2024, Sap et al. 2022)

**Partisan bias:** speech against one's own party judged more harshly — though evidence is mixed ([Pradel et al. 2024](https://www.cambridge.org/core/services/aop-cambridge-core/content/view/405333D7072585903E81BEF1729378F8/S000305542300134Xa.pdf/div-class-title-toxic-speech-and-limited-demand-for-content-moderation-on-social-media-div.pdf), [Rasmussen 2022](https://osf.io/preprints/psyarxiv/j4nuc), [Munzert et al. 2025](https://academic.oup.com/pnasnexus/article/4/2/pgaf032/8010228))

]

.pull-right[

## Myside bias: a structurally distinct form

Unlike identity bias, myside bias is **not a property of who the annotator is, but of what they believe** (Stanovich 2021)

- Operates **beyond party lines** and political domains
- Emerges **dynamically, issue by issue** — a person may side with a target on immigration but not on veganism
- **Cannot be corrected** by demographic reweighting or partisan-balance adjustments

Standard debiasing approaches calibrated on demographic or partisan dimensions will systematically miss this content-contingent component.

Community-based content moderation approaches that rely on self-selection into "like-minded" communities may exacerbate those effects!
]


---
# Pre-registered hypotheses

## Myside bias

**H1:** Respondents who **agree (vs. disagree) with the issue position of the target** are more likely to
- perceive messages as being hate speech and offensive speech and 
- prefer platform, legal, and employer action against the message/sender.


## Identity-based in-group/out-group bias

**H2:** Respondents who **share gender or ethnicity with the target** are more likely to
- perceive messages as being hate speech and offensive speech and 
- prefer platform, legal, and employer action against the message/sender.

**H3:** Respondents who **share gender or ethnicity with the sender** are less likely to
- perceive messages as being hate speech and offensive speech and 
- prefer platform, legal, and employer action against the message/sender.


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Setup
<html><div style='float:left'></div><hr color='#EB811B' size=1px style="width:1000px; margin:auto;"/></html>




---
# Data collection

.pull-left[
- Online survey fielded in 11 countries
- Recruitment via FB/Instagram ads
- ~18.5k completed interviews
- [Pre-registered at OSF](https://osf.io/2ec8m)

<br>
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/country-map.png" style="width:100%">
</div>
]

.pull-right[
<br>
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/facebook-ad-topic.png" style="width:45%">
<img src="pics/facebook-ad-neutral.png" style="width:45%">
</div>
]



---
# Vignette attributes and levels

.pull-left[
<div align="center">
<img src="figures/vignette-attributes-overview.png" style="width:100%; height: auto;">
</div>
]

.pull-right[
<div align="center">
<img src="figures/vignette-examples.png" style="width:90%; height: auto;">
</div>
]


---
# Some of the vignettes

<br><br>
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/vignettes-loop.gif" style="width: 80%; margin-top:-5px;"">
</div>


---
# Elicitation of preferences

.pull-left[
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/hate-survey-sample.png" style="width:70%; height: auto;">
</div>
]

.pull-right[

<div align="center">
<br><br>
<a href="https://simonmunzert.com/studies/myside-bias-vignettes" style="font-size: 1.5em; font-weight: bold;">https://simonmunzert.com/studies/</a><br>
<a href="https://digital-citizen-lab.com/hate-survey-sample" style="font-size: 1.5em; font-weight: bold;">myside-bias-vignettes</a>
<br><br>
<img src="pics/qrcode-vignettes-explorer.png" style="width:45%; height: auto;">
</div>
]



---
# Anchoring myside bias

<div style="margin-left: -2em; margin-right: -2em; padding-left: 0; padding-right: 0;">
<div align="center">
<img src="figures/barplot-tp-leftright.png" style="width:65%; height: auto">
</div>
</div>


---
# Perceived nature of speech and support for moderation

<div style="margin-left: -2em; margin-right: -2em; padding-left: 0; padding-right: 0;">
<div align="center">
<img src="figures/barplot-perceptions-actions-by-country.png" style="width:65%; height: auto;">
<img src="figures/profileplot-actions-by-perceptions.png" style="width:60%; height: auto;">
</div>
</div>



---
# Myside bias in content moderation I

<div style="margin-left: -2em; margin-right: -2em; padding-left: 0; padding-right: 0;">
<div align="center">
<img src="figures/effects-h1-3-coefplot-flipped.png" style="width:50%; height: auto;">
</div>
</div>


---
# Myside bias in content moderation II

<div style="margin-left: -2em; margin-right: -2em; padding-left: 0; padding-right: 0;">
<div align="center">
<img src="figures/effects-h1-coefplot-heterogeneity.png" style="width:75%; height: auto;">
</div>
</div>




---
# Respondent characteristics matter

<div style="margin-left: -2em; margin-right: -2em; padding-left: 0; padding-right: 0;">
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/respmodels-zoom.png" style="width:90%; height: auto;">
</div>
</div>


---
# Respondent characteristics matter

<div style="margin-left: -2em; margin-right: -2em; padding-left: 0; padding-right: 0;">
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/respmodels-zoom-2.png" style="width:90%; height: auto;">
</div>
</div>


---
# Zooming in: age and gender differences

<br><br>
<div style="margin-left: -2em; margin-right: -2em; padding-left: 0; padding-right: 0;">
<div align="center">
<img src="figures/effects-ageXgender-coefplot.png" style="width:85%; height: auto;">
</div>
</div>


---
# Zooming in: age differences across content types

<br><br>
<div style="margin-left: -2em; margin-right: -2em; padding-left: 0; padding-right: 0;">
<div align="center">
<img src="figures/effects-ageXcategory-coefplot.png" style="width:85%; height: auto;">
</div>
</div>

---
# Zooming in: gender differences across content types

<br><br>
<div style="margin-left: -2em; margin-right: -2em; padding-left: 0; padding-right: 0;">
<div align="center">
<img src="figures/effects-genderXcategory-coefplot.png" style="width:85%; height: auto;">
</div>
</div>



---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Time for a new agenda
<html><div style='float:left'></div><hr color='#EB811B' size=1px style="width:1000px; margin:auto;"/></html>

---
background-image: url("pics/fellow-kids.gif")
background-size: cover
background-color: #000000



---
# Online harassment: a problem of the young...

.pull-left[
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/pew-online-harassment.webp" style="width:60%">
</div>
]

.pull-right[
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/pew-online-harassment-5.webp" style="width:89%">
</div>
]

---
# ... and with variation across genders

.pull-left-small2[
<br>
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/pew-online-harassment-2.webp" style="width:100%">
</div>
]

.pull-right-wide2[
<br>
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/pew-online-harassment-4.webp" style="width:100%">
</div>
]


---
# Youth language and culture is different and changing fast

.pull-left[
<br>
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/know-your-meme.png" style="width:100%">
</div>
]

.pull-right[
<br>
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/langenscheidt-jugendwort.png" style="width:100%">
</div>
]


---
# We need to go places where this happens

<br>

.pull-left-small[
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/fortnite.png" style="height:400px">
</div>
]

.pull-left-small[
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/whatsapp.png" style="height:400px">
</div>
]

.pull-right-small[
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/4chan.png" style="height:400px">
</div>
]


---
# Towards a better understanding of youth speech preferences

.pull-left[
## Why this matters

- Language is identity for the youth; context (irony, memes!) is crucial
- Slang evolves faster than a millenial can scroll
- Platform use and communication styles are often gendered
- Current regulation rarely reflects young people's lived experiences
]

.pull-right[
## Where we need to go

- **Better data:** systematic mapping of how adolescents use and interpret toxic or provocative speech
- **Better ethics:** frameworks that take seriously the sensitivities of studying youth speech norms
- **Better dialogue:** between researchers, platforms, and young people themselves
]


---
# Data on toxic youth speech + moderation preferences

.pull-left[
## What we have
<br>
<div align="center">
<img src="pics/hate-speech-data-1-cr.png" style="width:90%">
<img src="pics/hate-speech-data-3-cr.png" style="width:90%">
<img src="pics/hate-speech-data-4-cr.png" style="width:90%">
</div>

**Plus:** Tools to generate synthetic content at scale, solid preference evaluation pipeline, comparative surveys on preferences (gender 👍, youth 👎)
]

.pull-right[
## What I'd like us to have
<br>
- Longitudinal behavioral **data on youth speech** and **platform use**
- **Platform moderation data**, ideally linked to user demographics
- **Everything all at once:** An ongoing, comparative, panel+cross-section youth survey infrastructure that integrates behavioral data and a module on speech tolerance
]
